metadata.title: Madison +1: Final Audit + Submit
metadata.playlist: Make It Visible — Visual Identity & Wireframes
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 7 (Visual) brief — Madison +1 audit, PDF export, Figma board, Canvas submit; due 2026-07-17

beats.narration_text: Hola — Liam, Episode 6 of 6. All the components are built: creative brief, logo, style guide, five desktop wireframes, mobile wireframe, user flow, platform decision. This episode is the Madison plus-one — the final question you ask before you submit. Does this actually look like the brand I described in my creative brief? Not 'does this look good.' Not 'am I happy with it.' Specifically: does each design decision match the brief I wrote at the start? That question is the plus-one, and it is not something nina can answer. It is your judgment call.

props.command: All components built. Run the Madison +1: does each design decision actually match the brief I wrote in Episode 1? Then export and submit.
props.runningText: 4-check audit against the brief · export checklist (PDF + PNGs + Figma link) · Canvas submit…
output: +1 question: 'Does this actually look like the brand I described in my creative brief?'
4 checks: logo ↔ adjectives · palette ↔ color direction · fonts ↔ inspiration · wireframes ↔ product
Submit: style guide PDF + wireframe PNGs + Figma board link — all 3 to Canvas

beats.narration_text: The Madison plus-one is a systematic pass through every design decision you made in this series, measured against the brief you wrote in Episode 1. The pass has four checks. Check one: does the logo activate the three adjectives from field four? Read the adjectives, look at the logo, answer yes or no. Check two: does the color palette match the color direction from field five? If your direction said 'institutional blues, no reds' and your palette has a red accent, flag it. Check three: do the fonts match the tone of the inspiration brands from field six? If your inspiration brands were minimal and editorial and your display font is ornate and decorative, that is a mismatch. Check four: do the five wireframe pages match the product described in field two? If your product is an AI meal planner and your wireframes show five enterprise SaaS pricing tiers, the wireframes are describing a different product. Four checks. Each is yes or no. Every no is a fix before you submit.

props.artifactHeading: Each check is yes or no. Every no is a fix before you submit.
artifactLines: Check 1: does the logo activate the 3 adjectives from field 4? (yes / no)
Check 2: does the palette match the color direction from field 5? No reds if you said 'no reds.'
Check 3: do the fonts match the tone of the inspiration brands from field 6?
Check 4: do the 5 wireframe pages match the product described in field 2?
Every no = fix before exporting. The machine audit finds systematic mismatches.
Your own eye finds the ones that technically pass but feel wrong.
props.sparkLine: The +1 question: does this actually look like the brand I described? Not 'does it look good.'

beats.narration_text: Export checklist. Three things to export before opening Canvas. One: the style guide as a PDF. Export from Figma or Google Slides — the grader will open this to check palette and fonts. Naming convention: LastName underscore FirstName underscore MadisonVisual.pdf. Two: the wireframe PNGs. Export each page frame from Figma at 1x — six PNGs total: five desktop pages and the mobile Home page. You can combine them into a single PDF or submit them as a ZIP — check the assignment brief for which format the grader prefers. Three: the Figma board sharing link. Set the board to 'anyone with the link can view.' Copy the link. Paste it into a text file and open it in a private browsing window to confirm it works before pasting it into Canvas.

props.artifactHeading: 3 items before Canvas. Verify the Figma link in private mode.
artifactLines: 1. Style guide PDF: export from Figma or Slides. Name: LastName_FirstName_MadisonVisual.pdf
2. Wireframe PNGs: 6 total — 5 desktop + 1 mobile Home. Export at 1x from Figma.
Combine into PDF or ZIP per assignment brief instructions.
3. Figma board link: set to 'anyone with the link can view.'
Paste into a private browsing window and confirm it opens before submitting.
Figma link failure (permission error) is the most common submission mistake.
props.sparkLine: Test the Figma link in private mode before Canvas. Permission errors are the most common failure.

beats.narration_text: Canvas submission for A7 Visual Identity. Two required files and one required link. File one: the style guide PDF containing the creative brief, logo concept rationale, color palette, font pairing, and style guide rules — all in one document. File two: the wireframe PNGs as a ZIP or combined PDF. Link: the Figma board, shared as view-only. After uploading all three to Canvas: refresh the submission page and confirm all three items show as attached. The rubric point for no submission equals no grade applies here the same as it does for the pitch video. The Figma link is the most common failure — students set the board to 'only people invited can edit' and the grader gets a permission error. Set it to 'anyone with the link can view' before copying the URL.

props.artifactHeading: 2 files + 1 Figma link. Refresh and confirm.
artifactLines: File 1: style guide PDF (brief + logo rationale + palette + fonts + usage rules).
File 2: wireframe PNGs as ZIP or combined PDF.
Link: Figma board — 'anyone with the link can view' (NOT 'only invited can edit').
After uploading: refresh Canvas submission page. Confirm all 3 show as attached.
'No submission = no grade' — same rule as the pitch video.
Figma permission error = grader sees nothing. Check the link before submitting.
props.sparkLine: Refresh Canvas after uploading. Confirm all 3 items show attached before logging off.

beats.narration_text: Series verdict. Six episodes. One visual identity. The arc: Episode 1 built the creative brief — six fields that drove every downstream decision. Episode 2 generated logo concepts and selected one with a rationale. Episode 3 built the mood board, palette, and style guide — the handoff document for any tool that builds the site. Episodes 4 and 5 built the wireframes — five desktop pages, one mobile page, one user flow, one platform decision. This episode ran the Madison plus-one and exported everything. The visual identity is not decoration — it is the brief that A8's website builder acts on. The clearer the style guide and the more specific the wireframes, the closer the A8 output will be to what you designed here. A loose brief produces a generic website. A specific brief produces the product you described.

props.artifactHeading: Series verdict — the whole visual identity arc
artifactLines: E1: creative brief — 6 fields that drive everything.
E2: 4–5 logo concepts → 1 selected, 1 rationale.
E3: mood board + Coolors palette + Google Fonts + style guide.
E4: 5 desktop wireframes in Figma (1440px, gray boxes, labeled).
E5: mobile Home + 5-step user flow + platform decision.
E6: Madison +1 audit → export → Canvas submit.
The style guide + wireframes are the A8 brief. Specific beats generic.

beats.narration_text: Your turn — the final prompt before submit. Here is the nina Madison plus-one audit prompt. Paste it with your complete A7 visual identity package in context. The prompt reads: Here is my complete visual identity package: creative brief, selected logo concept and rationale, color palette with hex values and rationale, font pairing, style guide rules, and five-page wireframe spec. Run the Madison plus-one audit: for each major design decision, check whether the choice matches what I wrote in the creative brief — specifically the three adjectives from field four, the color direction from field five, and the tone of the inspiration brands from field six. Flag any decision where the output drifts from the brief. Suggest one specific fix for each flag. Then generate a pre-submit checklist: style guide PDF naming, wireframe export format, Figma board sharing setting, Canvas submission confirmation steps. After running the prompt: fix every flagged drift before exporting. An unfixed flag is a point the grader will find. The machine audit finds systematic mismatches; your own eye finds the ones that technically pass but feel wrong.

props.command: Here is my complete A7 visual identity package: creative brief, logo concept + rationale, color palette with hex values + rationale, font pairing, style guide rules, 5-page wireframe spec. Run the Madison +1 audit: for each major design decision, check whether the choice matches the brief — adjectives (field 4), color direction (field 5), inspiration brand tone (field 6). Flag any drift. Suggest 1 specific fix per flag. Then generate a pre-submit checklist.
props.runningText: fix every flagged drift before exporting — an unfixed flag is a point the grader will find. Machine audit first, then your own eye for what technically passes but feels wrong…

beats.narration_text: Madison +1: Final Audit + Submit. Liam, in for Bear.
